title: Cat Trembling All Over: Causes and Urgent Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat trembling continuously? Learn about potential causes, when to seek emergency veterinary care, and how to help your pet right now.
content_html:
Understanding Continuous Body Trembling in Cats

Continuous, full-body trembling in cats is a distressing symptom that can stem from various serious conditions, including neurological disorders, exposure to toxins, severe pain, or metabolic imbalances. Unlike occasional shivering due to cold, continuous tremors suggest that the nervous system is under significant stress or that there is an involuntary muscle reaction to an internal medical crisis.

Urgency Level: High Emergency

Continuous full-body trembling is a high-priority medical emergency. This behavior often signals a life-threatening situation such as poisoning, hypoglycemia, or an impending seizure. Do not wait for the symptoms to pass; immediate veterinary intervention is required to stabilize your cat and identify the root cause to prevent permanent damage or loss of life.

Why a Video or Photo Helps Triage

If it is safe to do so, capturing a brief (10-15 second) video of the trembling can be an invaluable tool for your veterinarian. Cats often experience a surge of adrenaline during transport which may temporarily mask the tremors by the time you arrive at the clinic. A recording provides the medical team with a clear visual of the episode, helping them differentiate between tremors, seizures, or ataxia, ensuring a faster and more accurate diagnosis.

faqs.question: Could my cat just be cold?
faqs.answer: While cats do shiver when cold, continuous trembling that does not stop after being placed in a warm environment is typically a sign of a medical emergency rather than a temperature issue.

faqs.question: What are common toxins that cause cat tremors?
faqs.answer: Common household toxins include lilies, certain essential oils, human medications like NSAIDs, and flea treatments intended for dogs (containing permethrin) which are highly toxic to cats.

faqs.question: What should I do while transporting my cat to the vet?
faqs.answer: Place your cat in a secure carrier lined with a soft towel. Keep the car quiet and dark to minimize sensory stimulation, which can sometimes worsen neurological symptoms.
